critic_raw:
This multi-modal deep learning vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment has several critical methodological problems.

The most serious flaw is the test set leak in model selection. Section 6 explicitly states: 'After validation-based hyperparameter selection, a final check is run on the test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training on the combined train+validation set. This guards against validation-window overfitting.' This is a direct violation of test set integrity. Using the test set as a selection criterion — even for a 'final check' before committing to combined training — means the test set is used to make a modeling decision. Any performance reported on this test set is no longer a valid estimate of generalization. The justification ('guard against validation-window overfitting') is precisely the role of a validation set, not the test set.

The split strategy explicitly acknowledges a problem it does not fix: 'Partitioning will use stratified random sampling across all available years, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set... This approach aims to maintain class balance but may unintentionally mix future and past data in training and test sets.' The design admits temporal contamination but proceeds anyway. For a healthcare readmission model covering 2019–2023, the 2020–2021 COVID period materially changed readmission patterns; a random split that mixes COVID-period and pre/post-COVID patients across train and test produces an optimistic estimate of deployment performance for any one period.

The genomic features (rare variant burden by OMIM pathway, polygenic risk scores, copy-number variations) from a hospital-wide genomic sequencing database likely have a severe selection bias. Patients who receive genomic sequencing are not representative of all readmission-risk patients — they are typically patients with complex conditions, genetic concerns, or specific disease subtypes. Including only the 50,000 patients with complete EHR and genomic data from a single health system creates an inclusion criterion that makes the study population non-representative of general readmission risk, and the multi-modal model's advantage is demonstrated only in this highly selected subgroup.

The baseline logistic regression uses only 'demographic and clinical EHR features (no clinical notes or genomic data).' The multi-modal model uses three feature streams including clinical notes and genomic data. The comparison is not multi-modal integration vs. EHR-only; it is more data vs. less data. A fairer comparison would include a logistic regression or gradient-boosted model trained on all three feature types to isolate whether multi-modal deep learning architecture (joint embedding, shared layers) adds value beyond simply having more features.

For the multi-modal architecture, the three branch outputs are concatenated and passed through final dense layers. The design does not specify how missing genomic data is handled at inference time (e.g., for patients without sequencing). If the genomic branch receives zero-imputed features for patients without sequencing, the model may learn to treat zero-valued genomic features as a distinctive signal (indicating 'not sequenced'), which is a form of missingness as predictor — a legitimate technique but one that must be explicitly tested.
defender_raw:
This design tests multi-modal deep learning (EHR + genomic) vs. logistic regression on demographic/clinical features only for 30-day readmission prediction. The primary hypothesis is that genomic features provide additive predictive value over well-engineered clinical features.

The feature group structure is well-designed: EHR features (demographics, comorbidities, utilization, labs, discharge summaries) and genomic features (rare variant burden by pathway, polygenic risk scores) are treated as parallel modalities with separate branches, then concatenated. This architecture tests the hypothesis that genomic signals are complementary to EHR signals, not redundant. The baseline's exclusion of genomic data (and clinical notes) isolates the multi-modal contribution.

The inclusion criterion — all inpatients with complete EHR and linked genomic sequencing — introduces a significant selection bias that the design correctly acknowledges indirectly but does not fully address. Patients who have genomic sequencing are not a random sample of all readmission-risk patients; they are more likely to have complex chronic conditions, oncology diagnoses, or participation in research programs. This selection effect means the model's performance on the linked cohort may not generalize to the broader patient population. The defense is that this is a real-world constraint, not a design choice, and the scope statement should explicitly acknowledge this selection bias.

The preprocessing pipeline is correctly constructed: all transformers fit exclusively on training data and applied identically to validation and test. The clinical note vectorization using TF-IDF (vocabulary fit on training notes only) or frozen clinical BERT embeddings is appropriately specified — frozen embeddings avoid the risk of fine-tuning on a small readmission cohort that could overfit.

A notable concern: the design states 'a final check is run on the test set to verify the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training on the combined train+validation set.' This violates holdout discipline — any test set inspection before final evaluation allows implicit data-dependent selection of the final model configuration. This is a genuine methodological flaw. The design should use the validation set exclusively for all model selection decisions and reserve the test set for a single final evaluation.
all_issues_raised: The test set is explicitly used to make a modeling decision ('final check before committing to full training'), invalidating it as an unbiased generalization estimate — this is test set leakage by design.

The design acknowledges temporal contamination from the random split (mixing COVID and non-COVID periods) but proceeds without addressing it; readmission patterns during 2020-2021 are fundamentally different from other years.

Patients with complete genomic sequencing are a clinically selected, non-representative subgroup; the multi-modal model's advantage is demonstrated only in this highly selected cohort and cannot be attributed to multi-modal integration in general.

The baseline uses fewer feature types (no clinical notes, no genomics) than the multi-modal model; the comparison measures more data vs. less data rather than multi-modal architecture vs. equivalent single-modal model with all features.

Handling of missing genomic data for patients without sequencing at inference time is unspecified; zero-imputation would make missingness a predictive signal, which must be explicitly validated rather than treated as a neutral design choice.

The design acknowledges temporal contamination from the random split (mixing COVID and non-COVID periods) but proceeds without addressing it; readmission patterns during 2020-2021 are fundamentally different from other years.

Patients with complete genomic sequencing are a clinically selected, non-representative subgroup; the multi-modal model's advantage is demonstrated only in this highly selected cohort and cannot be attributed to multi-modal integration in general.

The baseline uses fewer feature types (no clinical notes, no genomics) than the multi-modal model; the comparison measures more data vs. less data rather than multi-modal architecture vs. equivalent single-modal model with all features.

Handling of missing genomic data for patients without sequencing at inference time is unspecified; zero-imputation would make missingness a predictive signal, which must be explicitly validated rather than treated as a neutral design choice.
